refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to duty cycle and site conditions, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation allows DPs to address enablement (e.g., responsive controls, efficient hydraulics, predictable motion) independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under controlled test conditions (agreed duty cycle, representative site conditions, nominal operator skill), the NGHE solution permits cycle execution within CT_target. Measure p95 cycle time across a representative sample of cycles. Verify that no design bottleneck (e.g., actuator response time, travel speed limit, dump mechanism dwell) prevents achievement of the target.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable load acquisition within the load phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable travel (loaded and unloaded) within the travel phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable material dump within the dump phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize transition delays between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into discrete, sequenced phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-gating logic that enforces strict ordering and allows independent optimization of each phase duration.
DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time controller that monitors elapsed time against phase budgets and dynamically adjusts operational parameters (speed, acceleration, dwell) to meet p95 target.

DP3: Design modular subsystem interfaces (powertrain, hydraulics, control) such that each subsystem exposes cycle-phase-relevant performance metrics (e.g., load-phase duration, travel speed capability) enabling predictive cycle-time calculation before execution.

DP4: Establish a duty-cycle characterization model (load mass distribution, travel distance, terrain, ambient conditions) and use it to pre-validate that nominal subsystem performance meets p95 target under worst-case site conditions.

DP5: Implement real-time cycle instrumentation and telemetry that captures phase transition timestamps and subsystem state, enabling post-execution analysis and continuous refinement of cycle parameters without design iteration.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP4 (integrated approach): Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into independently optimizable phases with strict phase-gating; design subsystem interfaces to expose cycle-relevant performance metrics; establish duty-cycle characterization and pre-validation to confirm p95 feasibility before deployment. DP5 (instrumentation) is a supporting enabler for continuous validation.

potential_sources: DP1 and DP3 are tightly coupled by design: phase-gating logic must consume subsystem performance metrics (e.g., powertrain acceleration ramp time, hydraulic flow rates) to enforce realistic phase budgets. This is acceptable coupling because it is intentional and supports the FR.

DP2 (dynamic adjustment) introduces feedback coupling: if selected, it would couple cycle controller to real-time subsystem state, increasing complexity and potential for oscillation. Recommend deferring DP2 to a secondary optimization layer after DP1+DP3+DP4 baseline is validated.
DP4 (duty-cycle characterization) is loosely coupled to DP1+DP3: it provides input assumptions but does not create runtime dependencies. This is healthy separation of concerns.

Risk: If subsystem interfaces (DP3) are not cleanly defined, phase-gating logic (DP1) will become tightly coupled to subsystem internals, violating independence. Mitigation: enforce interface contracts (e.g., 'powertrain shall report max acceleration capability') at architecture review.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP set avoids premature optimization (DP2) and instead establishes a predictable, decomposed baseline. Coupling is intentional and localized to the phase-gating ↔ subsystem-metrics interface, which is a natural architectural boundary.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Subsystem performance (powertrain, hydraulics, structural) is deterministic and repeatable under the agreed duty cycle. RISK: If subsystems exhibit high variability (e.g., hydraulic response time drifts with temperature), p95 target may not be achievable without active feedback control (DP2). MITIGATION: Characterize subsystem variability early; if σ > 5% of phase budget, escalate to DP2.

ASSUMPTION: Phase-gating logic can be implemented with low latency (< 10 ms) and does not introduce unintended delays. RISK: If control system has high jitter or communication delays, phase transitions may slip, violating p95. MITIGATION: Specify control system timing requirements explicitly; validate with hardware-in-the-loop simulation.

ASSUMPTION: Duty-cycle characterization (DP4) accurately represents worst-case site conditions (load mass, terrain, ambient). RISK: If actual site conditions exceed characterization envelope, p95 target will be missed. MITIGATION: Establish site survey protocol; require customer sign-off on duty-cycle model before deployment.

ASSUMPTION: Subsystem interfaces (DP3) are stable and do not change during development. RISK: If subsystems are redesigned (e.g., new powertrain), phase budgets must be recalculated. MITIGATION: version control subsystem interface contracts; establish change-impact assessment process.

ASSUMPTION: Cycle instrumentation (DP5) does not introduce overhead that degrades cycle time. RISK: If telemetry adds > 2% latency, it may mask or cause p95 violations. MITIGATION: design instrumentation as non-blocking; validate with profiling.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined cycle-time target (CT_target) is technically feasible given subsystem capabilities. RISK: If CT_target is unrealistic, no DP will succeed. MITIGATION: conduct feasibility study early; if gap exists, escalate to customer for target revision or subsystem upgrade.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: 'The NGHE solution shall execute the load→travel→dump cycle such that the 95th percentile cycle time, measured across ≥100 consecutive cycles under the characterized duty cycle and site conditions, does not exceed CT_target.' This clarifies the statistical nature of the acceptance criterion and supports DP4 (duty-cycle characterization). Additionally, add a secondary FR: 'The cycle control architecture shall enable independent optimization and validation of each phase (load, travel, dump) without requiring redesign of other phases,' which directly motivates DP1 and DP3. Finally, establish a gating criterion: 'Before hardware deployment, a validated duty-cycle model and subsystem performance characterization shall confirm that nominal operation meets p95 target; if gap > 5%, root-cause analysis and design iteration are required,' which operationalizes DP4.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates load→travel→dump cycle orchestration, phase-gating logic, duty-cycle characterization, performance metric exposure, and continuous instrumentation for p95 validation. Owns cycle state machine, phase transitions, subsystem coordination, and telemetry aggregation for cycle-relevant KPIs.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exposes cycle phase state, phase transition timestamps, performance metrics (load mass, travel distance, dump completion status), duty-cycle utilization, and p95 feasibility indicators to external monitoring and validation systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives cycle initiation commands (load, travel destination, dump parameters) and phase-gating constraints from mission planning or teleoperation layer.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety-critical phase transition failures, duty-cycle violations, or p95 threshold breaches that require immediate intervention or cycle abort.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication for remote duty-cycle pre-validation queries and real-time telemetry streaming during cycle execution.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Vehicle naturally owns the load→travel→dump cycle orchestration, phase-gating state machine, and duty-cycle characterization. Cycle-relevant performance metrics (load mass, travel distance, dump status) are intrinsic to vehicle subsystem state. Instrumentation (DP5) is tightly coupled to cycle execution and belongs in Vehicle. Minor diffusion: p95 pre-validation logic may require external duty-cycle database queries (via Network Link), but validation decision logic remains in Vehicle.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a command/control interface layer, not a cycle execution engine. Phase-gating and duty-cycle characterization are operational concerns, not teleoperation concerns. Placing cycle orchestration here would scatter phase logic across remote command handlers and local vehicle execution, creating tight coupling and diffusion. Teleoperation should consume Tasking interface to initiate cycles, not own cycle internals.

Weak fit. Safety Services should validate and gate cycle execution (via SafetyAlert), not orchestrate it. Placing phase-gating and duty-cycle logic here violates separation of concerns: safety validation is a cross-cutting concern, not the primary owner of cycle execution. High diffusion: cycle state, performance metrics, and phase transitions would need to be queried from Vehicle, creating bidirectional coupling and distributed state.

Very poor fit. NGHE Solution is a system-level integration boundary, not an execution module. Placing cycle orchestration here would create a monolithic, non-decomposable system. Phase-gating and duty-cycle logic are vehicle-specific operational concerns, not system-level concerns. Extreme diffusion: all cycle state and metrics would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural owner of the integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4 + DP5) because: (1) it executes the load→travel→dump cycle as its primary operational behavior; (2) it owns the subsystems (drivetrain, hydraulics, sensors) that generate cycle-relevant performance metrics; (3) phase-gating logic is intrinsic to cycle state management; (4) duty-cycle characterization is a vehicle-level operational constraint; (5) instrumentation for continuous validation is tightly coupled to cycle execution. The Vehicle module exposes cycle state and metrics via Telemetry, receives cycle commands via Tasking, reports safety violations via SafetyAlert, and queries pre-validation data via Network Link. This design achieves high cohesion (all cycle logic in one place) and low diffusion (external systems consume, not own, cycle behavior).
containment_risks: Risk: Duty-cycle pre-validation (p95 feasibility check) may require access to external historical data or fleet-wide statistics not available locally. Mitigation: Vehicle queries pre-validation database via Network Link before cycle initiation; validation decision logic remains in Vehicle.

Risk: Phase-gating constraints may be imposed by Safety Services or Teleoperation based on external conditions (e.g., terrain hazards, operator override). Mitigation: Vehicle receives phase-gating constraints via Tasking interface; Vehicle enforces them locally without delegating phase logic.

Risk: Telemetry streaming during cycle execution may create high-frequency network traffic. Mitigation: Vehicle aggregates cycle metrics and emits summary telemetry at phase boundaries, not continuously.

Risk: If Vehicle module becomes too large, cycle orchestration logic may need to be factored into a sub-module (e.g., CycleController). Mitigation: Ensure Vehicle remains the owning boundary; internal decomposition is acceptable as long as external interfaces remain stable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. The Vehicle IM boundary owns cycle orchestration, phase-gating, and duty-cycle characterization. Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target' is directly measurable via Telemetry interface (cycle phase timestamps, phase transition events, performance metrics). Pre-validation logic (DP4) queries external duty-cycle database via Network Link before cycle initiation, enabling p95 feasibility confirmation before deployment. Continuous instrumentation (DP5) aggregates cycle-relevant KPIs at phase boundaries, supporting post-deployment validation. Verification path is clear: (1) pre-deployment: Vehicle executes p95 feasibility check against duty-cycle constraints; (2) deployment: Vehicle emits phase transition telemetry and cycle completion metrics; (3) validation: external monitoring system correlates telemetry with CT_target acceptance criterion.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: EXCELLENT. FR allocation to Vehicle IM is unambiguous. Vehicle owns the load→travel→dump cycle as its primary operational behavior and naturally encapsulates all three DP components: (1) DP1 (phase decomposition + gating) is intrinsic to cycle state machine; (2) DP3 (subsystem interface design) exposes cycle-relevant metrics (load mass, travel distance, dump status) that Vehicle generates; (3) DP4 (duty-cycle pre-validation) is a Vehicle-level operational constraint check. External systems (Teleoperation, Safety Services, monitoring) consume cycle state via Telemetry and SafetyAlert; they do not own cycle logic. Ownership boundary is crisp and non-overlapping.

diffusion_risks: MINOR: Duty-cycle pre-validation (DP4) requires external database queries. Mitigation is sound (Vehicle queries via Network Link, retains validation decision logic locally). Risk is acceptable because decision logic remains in Vehicle; external data is a dependency, not a diffusion of responsibility.

MINOR: Phase-gating constraints may originate from Safety Services or Teleoperation (e.g., operator override, terrain hazards). Vehicle receives constraints via Tasking interface and enforces them locally. No diffusion of phase-gating logic; Vehicle remains the orchestrator.

MINOR: Telemetry streaming during cycle execution may create high-frequency network traffic. Proposed mitigation (aggregate metrics at phase boundaries) is appropriate. Risk is operational, not architectural.

ACCEPTABLE: If Vehicle module grows large, internal factoring into sub-modules (e.g., CycleController) is permissible as long as external interfaces remain stable. This is internal decomposition, not diffusion across IM boundaries.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle IM achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. All three DP components (DP1, DP3, DP4) are tightly coupled to cycle execution and naturally belong in Vehicle. DP5 (instrumentation) is a supporting enabler tightly coupled to cycle state and metrics generation; it belongs in Vehicle. No alternative IM candidate (Teleoperation, Safety Services, NGHE Solution) has comparable cohesion. Teleoperation (0.45 containment) is a command/control interface, not an execution engine. Safety Services (0.35 containment) is a validation layer, not an orchestrator. NGHE Solution (0.28 containment) is a system-level boundary, not an operational module. Vehicle is the clear, unambiguous owner. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and well-mitigated: external duty-cycle database queries are dependencies, not ownership diffusion; phase-gating constraints are inputs, not distributed logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4 + DP5 integrated approach) directly addresses FR. DP1 (phase decomposition + gating) enables independent optimization of load, travel, and dump phases, reducing cycle time variance. DP3 (subsystem interface design exposing cycle-relevant metrics) provides visibility into phase performance and enables real-time cycle monitoring. DP4 (duty-cycle pre-validation) confirms p95 feasibility before deployment, satisfying acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions.' DP5 (continuous instrumentation) supports post-deployment validation and continuous improvement. The integrated DP is logically sound, addresses all aspects of the FR (cycle time target achievement), and is traceable to acceptance criteria. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

recommended_revisions: CLARIFICATION: Explicitly document the duty-cycle pre-validation protocol. Specify: (1) what external data sources Vehicle queries (e.g., historical cycle time distributions, fleet-wide p95 statistics, site-specific terrain/weather data); (2) the decision rule for p95 feasibility (e.g., 'if predicted p95 ≤ CT_target with 95% confidence, proceed; else abort'); (3) the frequency of pre-validation (e.g., before each cycle initiation, or once per mission plan). This clarification strengthens DP4 traceability to acceptance criterion.

INTERFACE SPECIFICATION: Formalize the Tasking interface contract. Specify: (1) cycle initiation command schema (load mass, travel destination, dump parameters); (2) phase-gating constraints (e.g., max load mass, max travel distance, terrain restrictions); (3) timeout/abort conditions. This ensures Teleoperation and Safety Services correctly invoke Vehicle cycle logic without scattering phase-gating responsibility.

TELEMETRY SCHEMA: Define the Telemetry interface schema for cycle-relevant KPIs. Specify: (1) phase state enum (LOAD, TRAVEL, DUMP, IDLE); (2) phase transition timestamps (ISO 8601); (3) performance metrics per phase (load mass, travel distance, dump completion status, phase duration); (4) p95 feasibility indicator (boolean or confidence score). This enables external monitoring systems to validate acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target' without ambiguity.

SAFETY ALERT PROTOCOL: Clarify SafetyAlert interface. Specify: (1) alert types (phase transition failure, duty-cycle violation, p95 threshold breach); (2) severity levels (critical, warning); (3) recommended actions (cycle abort, operator intervention, teleoperation override). This ensures Safety Services correctly interprets and acts on Vehicle-generated alerts.

INTERNAL DECOMPOSITION GUIDANCE: If Vehicle module grows beyond a manageable size, provide guidance for internal factoring. Suggest: (1) CycleController sub-module owns phase state machine and phase transitions; (2) DutyCycleValidator sub-module owns pre-validation logic; (3) TelemetryAggregator sub-module owns metric collection and emission. Ensure all sub-modules remain within Vehicle IM boundary and do not create external diffusion.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERION OPERATIONALIZATION: Map acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' to measurable Vehicle outputs. Specify: (1) how p95 is computed (e.g., 95th percentile of cycle times over N cycles); (2) how duty cycle is characterized (e.g., load mass distribution, travel distance distribution, dump frequency); (3) how site conditions are captured (e.g., terrain type, weather, network latency); (4) how Vehicle reports p95 feasibility (e.g., via Telemetry or pre-validation query response). This ensures FR acceptance is verifiable and traceable to DP implementation.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence, and the Vehicle IM allocation is unambiguous. However, the recommended_revisions from the FR-IM gate expose critical specification gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Specifically: (1) duty-cycle pre-validation protocol lacks formalization (decision rule, data sources, frequency); (2) Tasking interface contract is underspecified (command schema, constraint types, abort conditions); (3) Telemetry schema is implicit, not explicit (phase state enum, KPI definitions, p95 feasibility reporting); (4) SafetyAlert protocol is vague (alert types, severity levels, recommended actions); (5) acceptance criterion operationalization is incomplete (p95 computation method, duty-cycle characterization, site condition capture). These gaps create ambiguity in DP realization and verification, which is unacceptable given high safety criticality. Revising the current level (DP and interface specifications) to formalize these contracts will eliminate ambiguity and enable unambiguous sub-FR decomposition in the next iteration. Proceeding to sub-FRs without this formalization risks scattering responsibility across sub-modules and creating diffusion at lower levels.

revise.why: DP1 (phase decomposition + gating), DP3 (subsystem interface design), DP4 (duty-cycle pre-validation), and DP5 (continuous instrumentation) are logically sound but operationally underspecified. Revision must formalize: (1) DP4 duty-cycle pre-validation protocol (external data sources, decision rule, invocation frequency, confidence thresholds); (2) DP3 subsystem interface contracts (Tasking schema for cycle initiation and phase-gating constraints, Telemetry schema for phase state and KPI emission, SafetyAlert schema for alert types and severity levels); (3) DP1 phase-gating logic (state machine transitions, timeout/abort conditions, constraint enforcement); (4) DP5 instrumentation scope (which KPIs are aggregated, at which phase boundaries, with what temporal resolution). These formalizations will create explicit, verifiable interface contracts that eliminate ambiguity in sub-module responsibility and enable clear traceability from acceptance criteria to DP implementation.

assumptions: Vehicle IM owns cycle orchestration and phase-gating logic; external systems (Teleoperation, Safety Services) are consumers, not orchestrators.
Duty-cycle pre-validation is a Vehicle-level operational constraint check; external database queries are dependencies, not ownership diffusion.
Phase-gating constraints originate from Teleoperation or Safety Services and are enforced by Vehicle via Tasking interface; Vehicle retains decision logic.
Telemetry streaming is aggregated at phase boundaries to mitigate network traffic; external monitoring systems validate acceptance criterion post-deployment.
Safety criticality is high; all phase transitions, constraint violations, and p95 threshold breaches must be logged and reported via SafetyAlert.
Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is measurable via Vehicle-emitted telemetry and pre-validation feasibility indicators.
Vehicle module may be internally factored into sub-modules (CycleController, DutyCycleValidator, TelemetryAggregator) without creating external diffusion, provided external interfaces remain stable.
open_risks: CRITICAL: Duty-cycle pre-validation decision rule is not formalized. If Vehicle queries external database but lacks a clear decision rule (e.g., 'proceed if predicted p95 ≤ CT_target with 95% confidence'), pre-validation may be inconsistent or non-deterministic. Mitigation: formalize decision rule and confidence thresholds in DP4 specification.

CRITICAL: Tasking interface contract is underspecified. If Teleoperation or Safety Services invoke cycle initiation without a clear schema (load mass, travel destination, dump parameters, phase-gating constraints), Vehicle may misinterpret commands or enforce constraints inconsistently. Mitigation: formalize Tasking schema and constraint types in DP3 specification.

CRITICAL: Telemetry schema is implicit. If external monitoring systems do not have a clear definition of phase state enum, KPI definitions, and p95 feasibility reporting, validation of acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target' may be ambiguous or non-reproducible. Mitigation: formalize Telemetry schema in DP3 specification.

HIGH: SafetyAlert protocol is vague. If Vehicle generates alerts without clear severity levels or recommended actions, Safety Services may not respond appropriately to phase transition failures or p95 threshold breaches. Mitigation: formalize SafetyAlert schema and alert types in DP3 specification.

HIGH: Acceptance criterion operationalization is incomplete. If p95 computation method, duty-cycle characterization, and site condition capture are not explicitly defined, verification of 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' may be subjective or non-verifiable. Mitigation: operationalize acceptance criterion in DP4 and DP5 specifications.

MEDIUM: Duty-cycle pre-validation frequency is not specified. If Vehicle queries external database before every cycle initiation, network latency may impact cycle start time. If Vehicle queries only once per mission plan, pre-validation may become stale. Mitigation: specify pre-validation frequency and caching strategy in DP4 specification.

MEDIUM: Phase-gating constraint sources are multiple (Teleoperation, Safety Services, terrain hazards). If constraints are not prioritized or conflict, Vehicle may deadlock or make unsafe decisions. Mitigation: specify constraint priority and conflict resolution in DP1 specification.
evidence_needed: Formalized duty-cycle pre-validation protocol: (1) list of external data sources (e.g., historical cycle time distributions, fleet-wide p95 statistics, site-specific terrain/weather data); (2) decision rule for p95 feasibility (e.g., 'if predicted p95 ≤ CT_target with 95% confidence, proceed; else abort'); (3) invocation frequency (e.g., before each cycle initiation, or once per mission plan); (4) confidence thresholds and fallback behavior.

Formalized Tasking interface contract: (1) cycle initiation command schema (load mass, travel destination, dump parameters, optional phase-gating constraints); (2) phase-gating constraint types (e.g., max load mass, max travel distance, terrain restrictions, operator override); (3) timeout/abort conditions (e.g., max cycle duration, max phase duration); (4) error handling and retry logic.

Formalized Telemetry interface schema: (1) phase state enum (LOAD, TRAVEL, DUMP, IDLE, FAILED); (2) phase transition timestamps (ISO 8601 format); (3) performance metrics per phase (load mass, travel distance, dump completion status, phase duration, subsystem health indicators); (4) p95 feasibility indicator (boolean or confidence score); (5) emission frequency (per phase transition, or periodic aggregation).

Formalized SafetyAlert interface protocol: (1) alert types (phase transition failure, duty-cycle violation, p95 threshold breach, subsystem fault, constraint violation); (2) severity levels (critical, warning, info); (3) recommended actions (cycle abort, operator intervention, teleoperation override, manual inspection); (4) alert emission conditions and thresholds.

Operationalized acceptance criterion: (1) p95 computation method (e.g., 95th percentile of cycle times over N cycles, with N ≥ 30); (2) duty-cycle characterization (e.g., load mass distribution, travel distance distribution, dump frequency, temporal patterns); (3) site condition capture (e.g., terrain type, weather, network latency, subsystem availability); (4) Vehicle reporting mechanism (e.g., pre-validation query response, telemetry KPI, post-deployment validation report).

Internal decomposition guidance: (1) CycleController sub-module specification (phase state machine, transition logic, timeout handling); (2) DutyCycleValidator sub-module specification (pre-validation logic, external data queries, decision rule); (3) TelemetryAggregator sub-module specification (KPI collection, phase boundary aggregation, emission frequency); (4) interface contracts between sub-modules (ensure no external diffusion).

Safety criticality assessment: (1) failure modes analysis (what can go wrong in phase transitions, duty-cycle validation, telemetry emission); (2) hazard severity assessment (impact of phase transition failure, p95 threshold breach, constraint violation); (3) mitigation strategies (redundancy, monitoring, alerts, operator intervention); (4) verification and validation plan (how to confirm safety requirements are met).
